core_concept: Motion is defined as the change in position of an object with time. In kinematics, motion is described without considering its causes, focusing on quantities like velocity and acceleration. For simplicity, objects are often treated as point masses when their size is negligible compared to the distance traveled.

Instantaneous velocity is the rate of change of position at a specific instant and is obtained as the limit of average velocity as time interval approaches zero. Speed is the magnitude of velocity and is always non-negative.

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time. It can result from changes in speed, direction, or both. Like velocity, acceleration can be positive, negative, or zero depending on the motion.

Graphs provide a visual representation of motion. The slope of position-time graph gives velocity, while slope of velocity-time graph gives acceleration. The area under a velocity-time graph represents displacement.
For motion with constant acceleration, a set of equations relates displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time. These equations simplify the analysis of motion without needing calculus.
Free fall is the motion of an object under the influence of gravity alone, neglecting air resistance. It is a case of uniformly accelerated motion with acceleration equal to g directed downward.

Stopping distance is the distance a vehicle travels before coming to rest after brakes are applied. It depends on the initial velocity and deceleration, showing a quadratic relationship with velocity.
Reaction time is the delay between observing a situation and responding to it. During this time, objects continue to move, and their motion can be analyzed using free fall equations.
